chapter: Chapter: On Equality (Social and Political Life-II)

questions.question: Universal Adult Franchise is a crucial aspect of democratic societies because it is based on the idea of:

options.B: Equality

questions.explanation: Universal Adult Franchise is based on the principle of 'one person, one vote', which establishes political equality among all citizens.

questions.question: Which of the following experiences made Kanta doubt the extent of equality in her life?

options.B: The ability to choose her representative.
options.C: Having to stand in a long queue at a government hospital while her employers never have to.
options.D: Being able to see her employer in the same voting line.

questions.explanation: While she has political equality (voting), the lack of access to quality healthcare due to her poverty shows economic and social inequality.

questions.question: In the matrimonial advertisements of English newspapers, we see that the concept of ______ is still important to highly educated urban Indians.

options.B: Caste

questions.explanation: Even in urban, educated settings, matrimonial ads often specify caste, showing that it remains a deeply rooted social structure.

questions.question: What specifically caused the 'violation of dignity' in the case of the Ansaris?

options.A: They were not allowed to look for apartments.
options.B: They were suggested to change their names to Mr. and Mrs. Kumar to get a house.
options.C: They did not have enough money to pay rent.
options.D: The apartment owners were their relatives.

questions.explanation: Being asked to hide their identity/religion just to get a house was an insult to their dignity and self-respect.

questions.question: Omprakash Valmiki's dignity was restored only when:

options.B: His father saw him sweeping and confronted the teachers.

questions.explanation: His father's intervention and refusal to let his son be treated unequally was the first step in challenging the violation of his dignity.

questions.question: The Indian Constitution recognizes every person as equal. This means that:

options.A: Every person has the same amount of money.
options.B: Inequality has completely ceased to exist in India.
options.C: All individuals are recognized as equal by the law, regardless of background.
options.D: Everyone must have the same job.

questions.explanation: Recognition of equality by the Constitution is a legal principle, even though social and economic inequalities may persist in reality.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four provisions of equality in the Indian Constitution?

options.A: Every person is equal before the law.
options.B: No person can be discriminated against on the basis of their religion.
options.C: Every person has access to all public places.
options.D: Every person must be given a government job.

questions.explanation: The Constitution guarantees equal opportunity, but it does not mandate that everyone must be given a government job.

questions.question: The practice of ______ has been abolished by the Indian Constitution to ensure equality.

options.B: Untouchability

questions.explanation: The abolition of untouchability is a specific constitutional provision to end a major form of caste-based discrimination.

questions.question: One of the two ways the government implements equality is through 'Government Programmes'. What is the primary aim of these programmes?

options.A: To punish those who are rich.
options.B: To help communities that have been treated unequally for several centuries.
options.D: To increase the number of elections.

questions.explanation: Government schemes are designed to provide opportunities to those who were historically denied them.

questions.question: In 2001, the ______ asked all state governments to begin the Midday Meal programme in their schools.

options.B: President

questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India directed all states to implement the Midday Meal scheme within six months.

questions.question: How did the Midday Meal scheme contribute to social equality?

options.A: It provided employment to Dalit women who were often hired to cook the meals.
options.B: It made school buildings bigger.

questions.explanation: By employing Dalit women as cooks, the scheme challenged caste barriers and provided economic opportunity.

questions.question: Despite the laws, why does inequality continue to exist in India?

options.A: The laws are not written properly.
options.B: Attitudes change very slowly and people still judge others by their caste or religion.
options.C: India is too large a country.

questions.explanation: Attitudinal change is slow; even though people know discrimination is against the law, they continue to practice it based on prejudice.

questions.question: African-Americans in the USA were treated extremely unequally. An example of this was:

options.A: They were not allowed to work.
options.B: They had to sit at the back of the bus or give up their seat to a white person.
options.C: They could not leave the country.
options.D: They had to pay more for food.

questions.explanation: Segregation in public transport was a major sign of inequality in the US before the Civil Rights Movement.

questions.question: Rosa Parks' act of defiance occurred on:

options.B: 26 January 1950

questions.explanation: On December 1, 1955, Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat, sparking the Montgomery Bus Boycott.

questions.question: What is the 'major challenge' for any democracy according to the chapter?

options.B: Collecting taxes.
options.C: The recognition of all persons as equal and maintaining their dignity.

questions.explanation: Ensuring that the promise of equality is fulfilled for every citizen remains the core challenge of democratic governance.

questions.question: Article 15 of the Constitution specifically prohibits discrimination on how many grounds?

options.B: Five

questions.explanation: It prohibits discrimination on grounds of: Religion, Race, Caste, Sex, or Place of birth.

questions.question: The term 'Dalit' is used to point out that even today, these people continue to be ______.

options.B: Seriously discriminated against

questions.explanation: The term literally means 'broken' and is used to highlight the systemic and ongoing nature of their discrimination.

questions.question: Why did the apartment owners in the city refuse to rent to the Ansaris?

options.B: The owners were prejudiced against their religion.

questions.explanation: Even though the Ansaris had money, the owners made excuses about meat-eating to hide their religious bias.

questions.question: According to the chapter, 'Dignity' refers to:

options.A: The amount of money one has.
options.B: Thinking of oneself and others as worthy of respect.

questions.explanation: Dignity is the inherent self-respect every human being deserves.

questions.question: The Midday Meal scheme helped mothers because:

options.B: They no longer had to interrupt their work to feed their children at home.
options.D: They didn't have to buy groceries.

questions.explanation: The scheme allowed working mothers to stay at their jobs without worrying about feeding their children during the day.

questions.question: When Omprakash Valmiki was sweeping the school, what was he 'denied'?

options.B: The right to sit with his classmates and get an education.
options.C: The right to wear a uniform.
options.D: The right to talk to his father.

questions.explanation: His forced labor replaced his education, violating his right to equal treatment in an educational institution.

questions.question: In the United States, most African-American children can only afford to attend ______ schools.

options.B: Government

questions.explanation: The disparity between underfunded government schools (mostly attended by African-Americans) and well-funded private schools shows economic inequality in the US.

questions.question: The struggle for equality in India is supported by:

options.B: The Constitution and the various movements of the people.

questions.explanation: The legal framework of the Constitution combined with active social movements keeps the fight for equality alive.

questions.question: The 'Civil Rights Movement' led to the passing of the Civil Rights Act in:
questions.explanation: The 1964 Act was the legislative victory of the movement, banning discrimination in public places.

questions.question: What is the primary message of B.R. Ambedkar regarding 'Self-respect'?

options.B: It is the most vital factor in life; without it, man is a cipher.
options.C: It can be bought with money.

questions.explanation: Ambedkar emphasized that living with self-respect is the most important human quality and is worth any sacrifice.
